questions.stem: Which sentence corrects the dangling modifier in "Walking to school, the rain began to fall"?
options: Walking to school the rain began to fall.
Walking to school, the rain began to fall heavily.
While I was walking to school, the rain began to fall.
The rain, walking to school, began to fall.
questions.explanation: A dangling modifier has no logical word to attach to. In the original, the phrase "walking to school" seems to describe "the rain," but rain cannot walk. The correct version names the real walker, so "While I was walking to school" gives the phrase a subject and removes the dangling modifier.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Identify the sentence that is free of ambiguity.
options: Visiting relatives can be tiring.
He fed the dog wearing his pyjamas.
She told her sister that she had won the prize.
After finishing the report, John submitted it to the editor.
questions.explanation: An ambiguous sentence can be read in more than one way. The first three each carry two possible meanings: it is unclear who won, whether the relatives or the visits are tiring, and who wore the pyjamas. The fourth sentence has only one clear reading, since "John" is plainly the one finishing the report and submitting it.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence corrects the misplaced modifier in "He almost drove the children to school every day"?
options: Almost he drove the children to school every day.
He almost drove the children to school every day.
He drove the children to school almost every day.
He drove almost the children to school every day.
questions.explanation: A modifier should sit next to the word it limits. In the original, "almost" sits before "drove" and suggests he nearly drove but did not. The intended meaning is that he drove on nearly every day, so "almost" belongs before "every day," giving "almost every day."
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which revision removes the dangling modifier from "To improve his grades, extra lessons were arranged"?
options: To improve his grades, extra lessons were arranged for him.
To improve his grades, he arranged extra lessons.
To improve his grades extra lessons were arranged.
Extra lessons, to improve his grades, were arranged.
questions.explanation: An introductory infinitive phrase must be followed by the person it describes. The original implies the lessons wanted to improve their own grades, which is illogical. The correct version names "he" as the subject right after the phrase, so the one improving his grades is the one arranging the lessons.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence places the word "only" to mean that she ate nothing except rice?
options: She only ate rice for lunch.
Only she ate rice for lunch.
She ate only rice for lunch.
She ate rice for lunch only.
questions.explanation: The word "only" limits whatever immediately follows it. To say that rice was the single food she ate, "only" must come directly before "rice," giving "only rice." Placing it before "ate" limits the action, and placing it before "she" limits the person.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence corrects the dangling modifier in "Having finished the exam, the hall felt quiet"?
options: Having finished the exam, the hall felt quiet to everyone.
Having finished the exam the hall felt quiet.
Having finished the exam, the students found the hall quiet.
The hall, having finished the exam, felt quiet.
questions.explanation: The opening phrase "having finished the exam" must describe whoever finished it. A hall cannot finish an exam, so the original dangles. The correct version supplies "the students" as the subject right after the phrase, since they are the ones who finished the exam.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence removes the ambiguity from "The teacher saw the boy with the telescope"?
options: The teacher saw the boy with the telescope.
The teacher saw the boy who had the telescope, with it.
Using the telescope, the teacher saw the boy.
With the telescope the teacher saw the boy with it.
questions.explanation: The original is ambiguous because "with the telescope" could describe either the seeing or the boy. Rewriting it as "Using the telescope, the teacher saw the boy" makes clear that the teacher held the telescope, removing the double meaning. The other options remain unclear or contradictory.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence corrects the misrelated modifier in "Covered in mud, the farmer washed the boots"?
options: The farmer washed the boots, which were covered in mud.
The farmer washed the boots covered in mud the farmer.
Covered in mud, the farmer washed the boots.
Covered in mud the farmer washed the boots.
questions.explanation: A misrelated modifier attaches to the wrong noun. The original makes it sound as though the farmer was covered in mud, when the boots were muddy. Recasting it as "the boots, which were covered in mud" links the description to the boots, where it belongs.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence is correctly constructed without a dangling modifier?
options: After reading the letter, tears filled her eyes.
After reading the letter, she wept.
After reading the letter, the news was sad.
After reading the letter, the sadness grew.
questions.explanation: The phrase "after reading the letter" needs a subject that can read. Tears, news, and sadness cannot read, so those versions dangle. "She wept" provides a person who can do the reading, so the modifier attaches correctly.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which revision corrects "Running across the field, my hat blew off"?
options: Running across the field my hat blew off.
My hat, running across the field, blew off.
As I ran across the field, my hat blew off.
Running across the field, my hat blew off in the wind.
questions.explanation: A hat cannot run across a field, so the original phrase dangles. Turning it into the clause "As I ran across the field" gives the running its true subject, the speaker, while the hat keeps its own verb, "blew off."
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence places the modifier "with a broken leg" so that it clearly describes the player?
options: The doctor examined with a broken leg the player.
With a broken leg, the player was examined by the doctor.
The doctor examined the player with a broken leg.
The doctor with a broken leg examined the player.
questions.explanation: To make the phrase describe the player rather than the doctor, it should sit next to "the player." Beginning the sentence with "With a broken leg, the player" places the phrase right before the player and removes the chance of attaching it to the doctor. The first option is ambiguous and the second wrongly gives the doctor the broken leg.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence corrects the dangling modifier in "At the age of six, my father took me to the farm"?
options: My father, at the age of six, took me to the farm.
At the age of six my father took me to the farm.
When I was six, my father took me to the farm.
At the age of six, my father took me to the farm.
questions.explanation: The phrase "at the age of six" should describe the speaker, but as written it seems to describe the father. Replacing it with the clause "When I was six" makes clear whose age is meant and removes the confusion.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence avoids the squinting modifier in "Students who study often pass the exam"?
options: Students who study pass often the exam.
Students who study often pass the exam.
Often students who study the exam pass.
Students who often study pass the exam.
questions.explanation: A squinting modifier sits between two things it could modify. In the original, "often" could describe either studying or passing. Moving it to "who often study" fixes its meaning to the studying, so the sentence reads clearly with a single meaning.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which revision corrects "To write well, good books must be read"?
options: To write well good books must be read.
Good books, to write well, must be read.
To write well, you must read good books.
To write well, good books must be read often.
questions.explanation: The infinitive phrase "to write well" needs a subject that can write. Books cannot write, so the original dangles. The corrected version names "you" as the writer right after the phrase, so the one writing well is the one reading good books.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence corrects the misplaced phrase in "The waiter served a meal to the guests on a silver tray"?
options: The waiter on a silver tray served a meal to the guests.
The waiter served a meal to the guests on a silver tray.
On a silver tray the waiter served a meal to the guests on it.
The waiter served the guests a meal on a silver tray.
questions.explanation: In the original, "on a silver tray" sits next to "the guests" and seems to place the guests on the tray. Moving the phrase so the meal is on the tray gives "served the guests a meal on a silver tray," which puts the modifier next to the meal where it makes sense.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence is free of a dangling modifier?
options: While sleeping, the alarm woke the baby.
While the baby was sleeping, the alarm woke her.
While sleeping, the alarm rang loudly.
While sleeping the alarm woke the baby.
questions.explanation: The phrase "while sleeping" must describe whoever was asleep. An alarm does not sleep, so the versions that follow the phrase with "the alarm" dangle. The correct sentence names "the baby" as the sleeper in a full clause, so the modifier attaches to the right subject.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence removes the ambiguity from "Flying planes can be dangerous"?
options: Piloting planes can be dangerous.
Dangerous flying planes can be.
Flying planes can be dangerous.
Planes flying can be dangerous to fly.
questions.explanation: The original is ambiguous because "flying planes" can mean the act of piloting or planes that are in the air. Rewriting it as "piloting planes" fixes the meaning to the activity of flying them, so only one reading remains.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which revision corrects "Reading in dim light, my eyes began to ache"?
options: Reading in dim light, my eyes began to ache badly.
My eyes, reading in dim light, began to ache.
Reading in dim light my eyes began to ache.
When I read in dim light, my eyes began to ache.
questions.explanation: Eyes cannot read, so the phrase "reading in dim light" dangles in the original. Turning it into the clause "When I read in dim light" gives the reading its real subject, the speaker, while the eyes keep their own verb.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence correctly places "that he bought yesterday" to describe the book?
options: He showed the book to his friend yesterday that he bought.
He showed that he bought yesterday the book to his friend.
He showed the book to his friend that he bought yesterday.
He showed the book that he bought yesterday to his friend.
questions.explanation: A relative clause should sit next to the noun it describes. In the first option the clause follows "friend" and seems to say he bought his friend. Placing "that he bought yesterday" right after "the book" makes clear the book was bought, which is the intended meaning.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence corrects the dangling modifier in "Being late, the gate was already locked"?
options: The gate, being late, was already locked.
Being late, the gate was already locked tight.
Being late the gate was already locked.
Because we were late, the gate was already locked.
questions.explanation: A gate cannot be late, so the phrase "being late" dangles in the original. Replacing it with the clause "Because we were late" supplies a subject that can be late and explains why the gate was found locked.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which version removes the ambiguous pronoun reference in "When Ama met Akua, she was wearing a red dress"?
options: When Ama met Akua, she was wearing a red dress.
When Ama met Akua she was wearing a red dress.
When Ama, who was wearing a red dress, met Akua, they greeted each other.
When she met Akua, Ama was wearing a red dress, she.
questions.explanation: The pronoun "she" in the original could refer to Ama or to Akua, so the reader cannot tell who wore the red dress. Recasting the description into a relative clause attached directly to Ama, "Ama, who was wearing a red dress," fixes the dress to Ama and removes the ambiguity. The other options keep or worsen the unclear reference.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which revision corrects "Driving through the village, the houses looked old"?
options: Driving through the village, the houses looked very old.
As we drove through the village, the houses looked old.
Driving through the village the houses looked old.
The houses, driving through the village, looked old.
questions.explanation: Houses cannot drive, so "driving through the village" dangles in the original. Rewriting it as "As we drove through the village" gives the driving a proper subject, the travellers, while leaving the houses to be the thing that looked old.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence places "nearly" to mean that the amount earned was close to one thousand cedis?
options: She nearly earned one thousand cedis last month.
She earned one thousand cedis nearly last month.
Nearly she earned one thousand cedis last month.
She earned nearly one thousand cedis last month.
questions.explanation: The word "nearly" should sit next to what it limits. To say the amount was close to one thousand, "nearly" must come before "one thousand cedis." Placing it before "earned" instead suggests she came close to earning but did not earn at all.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence corrects the dangling modifier in "To pass the interview, neat clothes are essential"?
options: To pass the interview neat clothes are essential.
To pass the interview, you should wear neat clothes.
To pass the interview, neat clothes are absolutely essential.
Neat clothes, to pass the interview, are essential.
questions.explanation: The infinitive phrase "to pass the interview" needs a subject that can pass an interview. Clothes cannot pass an interview, so the original dangles. The corrected version names "you" as the candidate right after the phrase, so the one passing the interview is the one wearing neat clothes.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide
